one_line_takeaway: Instead of hard-blocking flagged queries, GaaA has a lightweight guardian prepend a risk label plus explanation so the base model self-regulates under its own spec, cutting over-refusal (95% harmless accuracy) at competitive harmful-detection accuracy and only 2-10% latency overhead.
key_finding: GuardAdvisor reaches 90.52% average accuracy (95.08% harmless / 85.95% harmful), competitive with much larger closed-source guardians (GPT-4o 91.40% avg, GPT-4o-mini 88.16%) and open guardians (Granite-Guardian 90.57%, WildGuard 90.37%), while far exceeding Llama-Guard-3 (76.59%) and Llama-Guard-4 (79.28%) - and it does so under a harder task (also sub-classifying harmless into robustness vs honesty). More importantly for the paradigm's thesis, feeding GuardAdvisor's explanation back into the prompt raises response win-rate over the unaugmented prompt to 63.11% on robustness and 68.79% on honesty, beating advisory signals from GPT-4o (39.48%/54.47%) and GPT-4o-mini (46.11%/64.02%). The ablation shows the OOD-harmless design is what fixes over-refusal: SFT-only gives 68.99% harmless / 97.78% harmful, and progressively adding 1 OOD, 7 OOD, then category balancing moves this to 95.08% harmless / 85.95% harmful. Latency overhead is 2-10% end-to-end (GuardAdvisor detection ~22.1ms is <5% of base-model inference), scaling down with the harmful-input ratio.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Guardian-as-an-Advisor (GaaA), a 'soft-gating' guardrail paradigm for LLMs that, instead of forcing a static refusal, has a guardian model emit a binary risk label (Harmful/Harmless) plus a natural-language explanation and PREPENDS that advice to the original user query for a second inference by the deployed model, keeping the base model under its own model spec. Three concrete artifacts constitute the paper's OWN contribution: (1) the GaaA paradigm itself, contrasted with 'guardian-as-a-classifier' (hard gating) and 'guardian-as-an-explainable-classifier'; (2) GuardSet, a 208k+ multi-domain dataset unifying harmful and harmless cases with dedicated robustness and honesty slices, each instance pairing a binary label with a synthesized explanation; and (3) GuardAdvisor, a guardian model trained via SFT followed by GRPO reinforcement learning with an LLM-as-a-Judge reward that enforces label-explanation consistency. (Note: Llama Guard, Granite Guardian, ShieldGemma, WildGuard, Silent Guardian, Bergeron, Prompt Guard, RoboGuard, etc. are cited in Related Work as PRIOR systems, not contributions of this paper.)
threat_model: The guardian moderates interactions between a user and a deployed LLM without retraining the base model. The trustworthiness risks in scope are: safety-style harms (jailbreaks, toxicity/misuse, privacy leakage) mapped to a single 'Harmful' label, plus two pillars the authors argue prevailing safety-only taxonomies neglect even for benign inputs: robustness (natural noise such as typos, slang, spaced-uppercase/social-tagging perturbations) and honesty (model overstepping epistemic limits, e.g. claiming real-time data access or hallucinating under uncertainty). The paradigm targets the deployment-time tension where a conservative guardian's alignment goal conflicts with the base model's spec, causing over-refusal. The authors explicitly do NOT claim robustness against adaptive/adversarial evasion of the guardian; adaptive misuse and distributional drift are listed as open limitations.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: the dataset/evaluation cannot fully represent open-ended, evolving real-world interactions, so generalization to unseen or adversarial scenarios may vary; guarantees rely on approximate modeling and proxy (LLM-judge) assessments; the system remains exposed to adaptive misuse, distributional drift, and fairness concerns. Reviewer-observed caveats grounded in the text: (a) both dataset explanation synthesis and ALL accuracy/consistency metrics depend on LLM-as-a-Judge signals (DeepSeek-R1 for generation, GPT-4o-mini for validation and evaluation), so ground truth is model-derived rather than human-labeled beyond spot-checks; (b) the response-quality gains (Table 4) are win-rates judged against GPT-4o-mini responses on only robustness and honesty, not on refusal-relevant harmful cases, and use pairwise preference rather than absolute safety measurement; (c) harmful accuracy (85.95%) is notably lower than several baselines - the paradigm trades some detection strictness for reduced over-refusal; (d) soft-gating by design still lets the base model generate on flagged inputs, so its safety depends on the base model actually heeding the prepended advice.

datasets_used: GuardSet (the paper's own 208k+ corpus built from 55+ public sources; training total 200,314, test total 8,774 = 3,063 harmful + 5,711 harmless)

Jailbreak sources: WildJailbreak adversarial (50,000) and vanilla (20,000), JailbreakBench-artifacts (565), in-the-wild-jailbreak-prompts (1,558), ChatGPT-Jailbreak-Prompts (78), TrustGen jailbreak (596)

Toxicity/misuse sources: FredZhang7-toxi-text-3M (10,000), PKU-SafeRLHF-QA (5,827), Aegis-AI-Content-Safety-2.0 (2,598), TrustLLM-misuse (1,174), StrongReject (313), toxigen-data (1,007), lmsys toxic_chat, harmful-dataset (4,948), llm_attack_harmful_behaviors (520)
Robustness (perturbed harmless): bbh, mmlu, mnli, qnli, sst2, commonsense_qa, trivia_qa, truthful_qa, ultrachat, cnn_dailymail
Harmless/general: ai2_arc, alpaca-cleaned, gsm8k, math_instruct, medical_reasoning, mmlu, natural_instructions, openbook_qa, squad, trivia_qa, ultrachat, self_instruct, HotpotQA, SealQA, etc.
Deployed base models for latency/response study: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct
Pipeline models: DeepSeek-R1 (explanation synthesis), GPT-4o-mini (validation + LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation)
quantitative_results: Binary-classification accuracy (Table 3): GuardAdvisor 95.08 harmless / 85.95 harmful / 90.52 avg; GPT-4o 95.41/87.39/91.40; GPT-4o-mini 96.26/80.06/88.16; Granite-Guardian 92.07/89.06/90.57; WildGuard 91.67/89.06/90.37; Llama-Guard-4 64.35/94.21/79.28; Llama-Guard-3 57.08/96.09/76.59

GaaA response win-rate vs original prompt (Table 4): robustness GuardAdvisor 63.11 (tie 2.59) vs GPT-4o 39.48, GPT-4o-mini 46.11; honesty GuardAdvisor 68.79 (tie 3.18) vs GPT-4o 54.47, GPT-4o-mini 64.02
Training-setting ablation (Table 5): SFT only 68.99 harmless / 97.78 harmful; +1 OOD 75.24/95.95; +7 OOD 87.53/84.28; +balanced category 95.08/85.95
SFT-size trend (Fig 4a): as SFT grows 10k->150k, harmful acc rises 0.915->0.967 while harmless acc falls 0.841->0.757 (avg roughly flat ~0.86-0.88)
Inference time (Fig 4b): GuardAdvisor detection 22.1ms vs Llama 442.9ms and Qwen up to 1010.6ms, i.e. <5% of base-model compute
